name: Operationalist axiom
docstring: 1 (Operationalist axiom).  If a distinction cannot be witnessed by any discriminator in any \kappa-state, it is not a valid distinction within the framework. Equivalently: if no \kappa-evolution produces a discriminator that separates two claims, the claims are \kappa-equivalent and hence identical
label: def:operationalist

name: Discriminator
docstring: 2 (Discriminator).  A discriminator is a grade-1 element d \in \bigwedge^1 V together with a single predicate d: S \to \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2). The predicate fires (d(a) = 1) or is silent (d(a) = 0) on each element a \in S. A discriminator is to a property of the data and tests whether that property
label: def:discriminator

name: \tau and \sigma as datum properties
docstring: 3 (\tau and \sigma as datum properties).  \tau and \sigma are two properties that a datum may possess. They are attributes of the data, not of the discriminators. A discriminator keyed to \tau tests whether \tau is active for a datum; a discriminator keyed to \sigma tests whether \sigma is active. F
label: def:tau-sigma

name: Membership profile
docstring: 4 (Membership profile).  The profile of a datum a is the pair (\tau(a),\; \sigma(a)) \in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2) \times \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2), recording which properties have been recognized as active by the discriminators applied so far. Determining the full profile requires (at least) two discri
label: def:profile

name: The four-cell structure
docstring: 5 (The four-cell structure).  The membership profile takes values in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2) \times \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2), giving four possible states per datum: The four cells are the product of two independent properties of the data. An element is discriminated (ordered) when exactly one propert

name: Profile linearity
docstring:
6 (Profile linearity).  The map from discriminators to their predicates is linear: for discriminators d_1, d_2 \in
\bigwedge^1 V and any element a \in S, (d_1 + d_2)(a) = d_1(a) + d_2(a), where addition on the left is in V and on the right is in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2). This is a structural axiom: t
label: def:profile-linear

name: Definition
docstring: 7.  Profile linearity (Definition ) is not a free consequence of the definitions — it is an explicit requirement that ties the algebraic structure of V to the operational behavior of discriminators. Without it, discriminators would be elements of V algebraically but their predicates could behave non

name: XOR signature
docstring: 8 (XOR signature).  The signature of an element a under a discriminator pair (d_\tau, d_\sigma) is \chi(a) = d_\tau(a) + d_\sigma(a) \in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2). When \chi(a) = 1, exactly one predicate fires: the element is discriminated (ordered). When \chi(a) = 0, both fire or neither fires: the e

name: Residue
docstring: 9 (Residue).  The residue of a discriminator pair (d_\tau, d_\sigma) applied to a collection S is R(S) = \{ a \in S \mid \chi(a) = 0 \}. These are the elements for which discrimination was non-trivial in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2): 1 + 1 = 0 (overlap) or 0 + 0 = 0 (gap).
label: def:residue

name: Two origins of residue
docstring: 10 (Two origins of residue).  Gap residues (\tau = \sigma = 0) arise from novelty: the element has no membership under either predicate. Overlap residues (\tau = \sigma = 1) arise from abstraction: the element is above the discriminator’s resolution, as when presenting mammal to a cat/dog discrimina
label: rem:residue-origins

name: Discrimination regime
docstring: 11 (Discrimination regime).  A regime \kappa is a finite, ordered sequence of discriminators (d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n), each providing a single predicate d_i: S \to \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2). The dimensionality of \kappa is n. The \tau/\sigma pairing structure (Definition ) is imposed on pairs of discri
label: def:kappa

name: \kappa-evolution
docstring: 12 (\kappa-evolution).  A -evolution is the extension of \kappa by a new discriminator d_{n+1}, producing \kappa' = (d_1, \ldots, d_n, d_{n+1}). Evolution is triggered by the articulation of a predicate, which is simultaneously the construction of a residue via the wedge product.
label: def:evolution

name: Monotonicity of \kappa
docstring: 13 (Monotonicity of \kappa).  removed.
label: prop:monotone

name: Write path
docstring: 14 (Write path).  The path is the \kappa-evolution process: each residue is consumed exactly once to produce exactly one new dimension.

name: Read path
docstring: 15 (Read path).  The path is the process of evaluating discriminator predicates over a collection S given a fixed \kappa. Each discriminator d_i produces one bit d_i(a) \in \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2) per element. For a \tau/\sigma pair (d_\tau, d_\sigma) within \kappa, the read path produces the member

name: Order-independence of filtration
docstring: 16 (Order-independence of filtration).  order.
label: prop:monoidal

name: Well-definedness of type erasure
docstring: 17 (Well-definedness of type erasure).  filtration.

name: Membership
docstring: 18 (Membership).  An element a is a member of a collection S under a \tau/\sigma pair (d_\tau, d_\sigma) in regime \kappa, written a \in_{(d_\tau, d_\sigma),\kappa} S, if d_\tau(a) = 1 (the \tau-discriminator fires on a). The element is in the complement if d_\sigma(a) = 1. Membership is a relation
label: def:membership

name: Yoneda content
docstring: 19 (Yoneda content).  That membership depends on the collection — not just the element and the discriminator — reflects the Yoneda perspective: the identity of an element is constituted by its predicate profile against the entire population under \kappa-filtration.

name: \kappa-equivalence
docstring: 20 (\kappa-equivalence).  Two collections A and B are -equivalent, written A \equiv_\kappa B, if for every discriminator d in \kappa and every element a in the ambient population, (\tau_d(a), \sigma_d(a)) computed over A equals (\tau_d(a), \sigma_d(a)) computed over B.

name: Extensionality
docstring: 21 (Extensionality).  classes.
label: thm:ext

docstring: 22.  Extensionality in ZFC states that two sets are equal iff they have the same members. Here the same members condition is relativized to \kappa: two sets are equal regime. A finer \kappa may distinguish sets that a coarser \kappa identifies. Extensionality is \kappa-relative, which is strictly mo

name: Discriminative comprehension
docstring: 23 (Discriminative comprehension).  For any predicate P that can be articulated as a discriminator, the act of articulation is a \kappa-evolution producing a new discriminator d_P. The set \{a \in S : P(a)\} is the collection \{a \in S : \tau_{d_P}(a) = 1\} (or \sigma_{d_P}(a) = 1, depending on the
label: def:comprehension

name: Evaluation linearity
docstring: 25 (Evaluation linearity).  For each discriminator x \in V, the \tau-profile \tau_x: V \to \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2) is a \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2)-linear functional when restricted to S \cap V. Together with profile linearity (Definition ), this makes the profile pairing B(x,y) = \tau_x(y) a bilinear f
label: def:eval-linear

name: Profile linearity as the load-bearing axiom
qualname: remark:Profile linearity as the load-bearing axiom
docstring: 26 (Profile linearity as the load-bearing axiom).  The dissolution depends on profile linearity (Definition ), not on x \wedge x = 0 directly. The wedge product’s role is indirect: x \wedge x = 0 ensures that B(x,x) is linear over \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2), but the Russell predicate’s constant offset

name: The restriction is relocated
docstring: 27 (The restriction is relocated).  Profile linearity (Definition ) is a axiom: it restricts what counts as a valid discriminator, just as ZFC’s separation axiom restricts what counts as a valid set-formation. The Russell predicate is not algebraically degenerate in the exterior algebra — it is -pro

name: General pathology protection
docstring: 28 (General pathology protection).  One may ask whether the framework might be vulnerable to set-theoretic pathologies beyond Russell’s. The framework handles pathologies via two mechanisms: Specific pathologies are handled by: comprehension bounded by profile linearity (Definition ), foundation by

name: Boolean dimension
docstring: 29 (Boolean dimension).  A dimension d of \kappa is S if for every a \in S, \chi_d(a) = 1 — that is, exactly one of the two predicates fires for every element (\tau_d(a) = 1 and \sigma_d(a) = 0, or vice versa). The two independent partitions happen to be complementary on S: every element \tau-compre
label: def:boolean-dim

docstring: 31.  This is perhaps the central philosophical claim: classical logic’s excluded middle is not a law of thought but a theorem, local to specific \kappa-dimensions over specific collections, and it must be verified constructively. Boolean complementation requires a mechanism; it is not free.

name: Cycles of length \geq 2 are permitted
docstring: 33 (Cycles of length \geq 2 are permitted).  The framework does not exclude membership cycles of length \geq 2. A cycle a \in b \in a can be witnessed by two independent discriminators d_1, d_2 with \tau_{d_1}(a) = 1, \sigma_{d_1}(b) = 1, \tau_{d_2}(b) = 1, \sigma_{d_2}(a) = 1. This is algebraically
label: rem:cycles

name: by finiteness of \kappa
docstring: 34.  In ZFC, Foundation and the axiom of dependent choice together prevent both infinite descending chains and finite cycles. The framework prevents infinite chains (by finiteness of \kappa) and self-membership (by profile linearity (Definition )), but permits finite cycles. This more permissive str

name: Theorem
docstring: 35.  In ZFC, foundation is an axiom imposed to prevent pathologies such as x \in x. Here, x \in x is already excluded by x \wedge x = 0 (Theorem ), and the more general well-foundedness condition follows from the irreversibility of \kappa-evolution. Foundation is a consequence of profile linearity (

40.  This reversal of the usual ZFC presentation — where union is axiomatic and intersection is defined via comprehension — is a direct consequence of working over \mathop{\mathrm{GF}}(2) rather than Boolean algebra.

name: Discriminative power set
docstring: 41 (Discriminative power set).  The set of a collection S under regime \kappa is the set of all sub-collections of S obtainable by \kappa-deformations (projections to sub-regimes). If \kappa has n dimensions, the power set has at most 2^n elements.
label: def:powerset

name: \mathbb{N}
docstring: 42.  The power set is not a fixed Platonic totality. It is bounded by the current \kappa-state and grows as \kappa evolves. This gives power sets a constructive character absent from ZFC, where \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N}) has a fixed (if undetermined) cardinality.

name: Constructive continuum
docstring: 43 (Constructive continuum).  In the discrimination framework, the cardinality of the power set of \mathbb{N} is not a fixed value but an open-ended function of the discrimination regime, growing with each new predicate articulated over \mathbb{N}. This suggests a correspondence with the independenc
label: conj:CH

name: Discriminative infinity
docstring: 44 (Discriminative infinity).  An collection is a collection S such that for every \kappa-state, there exists a predicate whose articulation (and therefore whose \kappa-evolution) produces a non-trivial residue over S. That is: no finite \kappa renders S fully discriminated.
label: def:infinity

name: Indistinguishability of potential and actual infinity
docstring: 45 (Indistinguishability of potential and actual infinity).  claims: ).

docstring: 46.  This does not deny the usefulness of reasoning about completed infinities. Classical mathematics routinely and productively treats the natural numbers, the reals, and higher cardinalities as completed totalities. The framework accommodates this by the same mechanism it accommodates all predicat

name: n,1
docstring: 100.  The (n,1)-structure is not a design choice. It follows from the architecture: \tau/\sigma labels the population; grade 1 elements contact the population; grade \geq 2 elements do not. Direction exists exactly where the algebra meets the world.

name: n,1
docstring: 103.  The symmetry of the 2-cell means there is no preferred direction of the homotopy between f and g. The two directed paths are identified without one being before or after the other. This is the correct behavior for an (n,1)-category: 2-morphisms are equivalences, not directed transformations.

name: Evidence semantics
docstring: 141 (Evidence semantics).  \tau_d(a) = 1 is a positive assertion that \tau fires. \tau_d(a) = 0 is silence, not falsehood. The value 0 reports no match, not false.

name: Information ordering
docstring:
142 (Information ordering).  The Belnap lattice: (0,0) \sqsubseteq \{(1,0), (0,1)\}
\sqsubseteq (1,1). Gap = minimal information. Overlap = maximal (conflicting) information. Boolean states = stable middle.
label: def:info-order
